1.title: TOM AND AUNT POLLY
1.text:
"Tom!"
No answer.
"Tom!"
Still nothing.
"Where has that boy gone? You, Tom!"
The old woman pulled her glasses down and peered over them, scanning the room. Then she pushed them up and looked underneath them. She almost never looked through them—they were her fancy pair, meant for style, not for actually seeing anything. She could have seen just as well through a pair of oven doors.
She looked annoyed but not truly angry. "Well, I swear, if I catch you, I'll—"
She didn't finish, because she was already on her hands and knees, jabbing a broom handle under the bed. She found nothing but the cat.
"I have never seen a boy like this one!"
She marched to the open door and looked out at the tangled garden—tomato vines, weeds growing every which way. No Tom. She took a deep breath and hollered toward the distance: "Y-o-u-u, Tom!"
A small noise came from behind her. She spun around just in time to grab a boy by the back of his shirt before he could escape.
"There you are! What have you been doing in that closet?"
"Nothing."
"Nothing! Look at your hands. And your mouth. What is that stuff?"
"I don't know, Aunt Polly."
"Well, I know. It's jam. I have told you forty times—if you don't leave that jam alone, I will skin you alive. Hand me that switch."
The switch hovered in the air. Things looked bad—
"My! Look behind you, Aunt!"
The old woman spun around, grabbing at her skirts. In that instant, the boy bolted. He scrambled up the tall board fence and vanished over the other side.
His Aunt Polly stood there a moment, surprised, and then laughed softly.
"That boy! Can't I ever learn? He's played that same trick on me enough times that I ought to know better. But he never does it the same way twice, so how am I supposed to keep up? He knows exactly how far he can push me before I lose my temper, and he knows if he can make me laugh, I can't bring myself to punish him. I'm not doing right by that boy, and that's the honest truth. But he's my dead sister's child, and I haven't got the heart to whip him. Every time I let him off, my conscience hurts me, and every time I do punish him, my old heart nearly breaks. Well—he'll skip school this afternoon, so I'll have to make him work tomorrow as punishment. Making him work on a Saturday, when all the other boys are free—that's the hardest thing in the world for him. But I've got to do my duty, or I'll be the ruin of that child."
Tom did skip school, and he had a fine time doing it. He got home barely in time to help Jim, the hired boy, chop wood for supper—or at least he was there in time to tell Jim about his adventures while Jim did most of the work.
Tom's younger half-brother, Sid, was already done with his chores. Sid was a quiet boy who never caused trouble and never looked for any.
While Tom ate supper and snuck sugar whenever he got the chance, Aunt Polly asked him tricky questions, trying to catch him in a lie. She fancied herself a clever detective. She said:
"Tom, it was pretty warm in school today, wasn't it?"
"Yes ma'am."
"Very warm, wasn't it?"
"Yes ma'am."
"Didn't you want to go swimming?"
A flicker of alarm crossed Tom's face. He studied his aunt's expression, but she gave nothing away. "No ma'am—well, not too much."
Aunt Polly reached out and felt his shirt. "But you don't seem too warm now." She was pleased with herself for checking whether the shirt was dry without anyone knowing that was her plan. But Tom was onto her, so he got ahead of the next move.
"Some of us pumped water on our heads at school. Mine's still a little damp. See?"
Aunt Polly was annoyed she'd missed that detail. Then she had a new idea: "Tom, you didn't have to undo the collar I sewed shut to pump water on your head, did you? Unbutton your jacket!"
Tom opened his jacket. His shirt collar was securely sewn shut.
"Hmph! All right, go on. I was sure you'd skipped school and gone swimming. But I forgive you, Tom. I suppose you're better than you look."
She was half sorry her detective work had failed and half glad that Tom had actually behaved for once.
But then Sid spoke up. "Funny—I thought you sewed that collar with white thread, Aunt Polly. But it's black."
"Why, I did sew it with white! Tom—"
But Tom was already out the door. "I'll get you for that, Sid," he called back.
In a safe spot, Tom examined two needles stuck into his jacket—one threaded with white, one with black. He'd switched the thread before coming home.
"She'd never have noticed if Sid hadn't opened his big mouth," Tom muttered. "Sometimes she uses white thread and sometimes black. I wish she'd pick one and stick with it. But I will get even with Sid."
Tom was not the model boy of the village. He knew who the model boy was, though—and hated him.
Within two minutes, Tom had forgotten every one of his troubles. Not because they were small troubles—they felt just as heavy to him as a grown man's worries feel to a grown man. But a new interest had pushed everything else aside. He'd just learned a new way of whistling from one of the older boys in town, and he was dying to practice it.
The summer evening was long, and it wasn't dark yet. As Tom walked down the street, whistling happily, he spotted a stranger—a boy about his own size. A newcomer in the small village of St. Petersburg was a rare and impressive sight. This boy was well-dressed, too, which was downright amazing for a weekday. He wore a neat cap, a buttoned-up jacket, nice pants, and actual shoes—on a Friday! He even had a necktie. Everything about him screamed city boy.
The fancier this stranger looked, the shabbier Tom felt. Neither boy spoke. They circled each other like cats.
Finally Tom said, "I can lick you."
"I'd like to see you try."
"Well, I can do it."
"No you can't."
"Yes I can."
"Can't."
"Can!"
There was an uncomfortable pause. Tom said, "What's your name?"
"None of your business."
"I'll make it my business."
"I dare you to try."
"You think you're something special, don't you? What a stupid hat."
"You can knock it off if you don't like it."
"You're a liar."
"You're another."
"You're a fighting liar and too scared to back it up."
"Get away from here!"
"Go away yourself."
"I won't."
"I won't either."
They stood nose to nose, each with one foot braced, shoving with all their might. Neither could budge the other. Both were red-faced and sweating.
Tom drew a line in the dirt with his toe. "Step over that line, and I'll fight you till you can't stand up."
The new boy stepped over it immediately.
"Now you said you would—so do it!"
"Don't push me."
"Well, do it!"
"For two cents, I would."
The new boy dug two pennies out of his pocket and held them out. Tom smacked them to the ground.
In the next instant, both boys were rolling in the dirt, punching and pulling hair, covered in dust. When the dust settled, Tom was sitting on top of the new boy, hammering him with his fists.
"Say uncle!"
The boy struggled, crying—mostly from rage.
"Say uncle!"
At last the stranger choked out: "Uncle!"
Tom let him up. "That'll teach you. Better watch who you mess with."
The new boy slunk off, crying and threatening revenge. Tom jeered after him and started walking home, feeling like a champion. The moment Tom turned his back, the new boy snatched up a rock and hurled it, catching Tom right between the shoulder blades. Then he ran.
Tom chased the boy all the way home and stood outside his gate for a long time, daring him to come out. But the boy only made faces through the window. Finally the boy's mother appeared, called Tom a wicked, vulgar child, and ordered him away.
Tom got home late that night. When he climbed in through the window, he ran straight into an ambush: Aunt Polly was waiting. When she saw the state of his clothes, she made up her mind. His Saturday holiday was about to become a Saturday of hard labor—and there would be no getting out of it.

2.title: THE WHITEWASHING
2.text:
Saturday morning came, bright and golden. The world was alive with summer—birds singing, flowers blooming, the sweet smell of blossoms in the air. Cardiff Hill rose green and inviting beyond the village, looking like a picture from a dream.
And there stood Tom on the sidewalk with a bucket of whitewash and a long-handled brush.
He stared at the fence. It was thirty yards long and nine feet high. All the happiness drained out of him. Life felt pointless. He sighed, dipped the brush, painted a single plank, then did it again. He looked at the tiny white streak compared to the vast unpainted fence, and sat down on a box, completely defeated.
Jim came through the gate, singing on his way to fetch water from the town pump. Tom had always thought fetching water was miserable work, but it didn't look so bad now—at least there would be other kids at the pump to talk to.
"Hey Jim, I'll get the water if you'll whitewash a little."
Jim shook his head. "Can't, Mister Tom. Your aunt told me to fetch this water and not fool around with anybody. She said she figured you'd try to get me to whitewash, and she told me to mind my own business. She said she'd handle the whitewashing herself."
"Oh, never mind what she said. She always talks like that. Give me the bucket—I'll only be a minute. She won't ever know."
"I don't dare. She'd knock me sideways."
Tom tried a white marble, his sore toe, and every trick he could think of. Jim was weakening—but then Aunt Polly appeared out of nowhere, smacked Jim with her slipper, and sent him running. Tom went back to whitewashing, and Aunt Polly marched away, satisfied.
Tom's energy didn't last long. He started thinking about all the fun things he'd planned for today, and his mood sank lower. Soon the free boys would come walking past on their way to all kinds of adventures, and they would laugh at him for having to work. The thought burned like fire.
He pulled out everything he owned—bits of toys, marbles, and junk. Not nearly enough to buy even half an hour of freedom from another boy. And then, at this dark moment, a magnificent idea hit him. A brilliant, world-class idea.
He picked up his brush and went calmly back to work.
Ben Rogers appeared around the corner—the very boy whose teasing Tom had dreaded most. Ben was prancing along, eating an apple, and pretending to be a steamboat. He was making engine noises, shouting orders to imaginary crewmen, and having a grand time.
Tom kept painting. He didn't even look up.
Ben stared. "Ha! You're stuck working, aren't you!"
Tom turned, as if surprised. "Oh, it's you, Ben. I didn't notice."
"I'm going swimming. Don't you wish you could come? But I guess you'd rather work, huh?"
Tom studied the boy for a moment and said, "What do you call work?"
"What do you mean? Isn't that work?"
Tom went back to painting, nice and slow, like an artist. "Well, maybe it is, and maybe it isn't. All I know is, it suits Tom Sawyer."
"Oh, come on. You don't mean you actually like it?"
The brush kept moving. "Like it? Well, why shouldn't I? Does a boy get a chance to whitewash a fence every day?"
That put everything in a new light. Ben stopped chewing his apple. Tom swept his brush carefully back and forth—stepped back to examine the result—added a touch here, fixed a spot there. Ben watched every move, getting more and more fascinated. Finally he said:
"Hey Tom, let me whitewash a little."
Tom thought about it, then shook his head. "No, I'd better not. Aunt Polly is real particular about this fence—it's right out front, you know. If it was the back fence, I wouldn't care. But this one has to be done just right. I bet there isn't one boy in a thousand who could do it the way it needs to be done."
"Oh, come on, let me try. I'll be careful. I'll give you the core of my apple."
"Well—no, Ben, I'm afraid—"
"I'll give you all of it!"
Tom handed over the brush with a show of reluctance but joy in his heart. And while Ben worked and sweated in the sun, Tom sat in the shade on a barrel, munching the apple and planning his next move.
There was no shortage of victims. Boys came by every little while. They came to mock, but they stayed to whitewash. By the time Ben was worn out, Tom had traded the next turn to Billy Fisher for a kite. When Billy was done, Johnny Miller bought a turn for a dead rat on a string. And so it went, hour after hour.
By mid-afternoon, Tom had gone from a boy with nothing to a boy rolling in treasure. He had: twelve marbles, a piece of blue glass, a tin soldier, a couple of tadpoles, six firecrackers, a one-eyed kitten, a brass doorknob, a dog collar (but no dog), the handle of a knife, and four pieces of orange peel.
And the fence had three coats of whitewash on it.
Tom had discovered a great law of human nature: in order to make someone want something, all you have to do is make it hard to get.

3.title: SHOWING OFF FOR BECKY
3.text:
After church on Sunday, Tom sat through a long, boring sermon. The only interesting moment came when a stray poodle wandered down the aisle and sat on a large beetle. The resulting yelp sent the dog flying around the church like a furry comet, and the whole congregation nearly suffocated trying not to laugh. The sermon never recovered, and Tom went home in a fine mood.
Monday morning found Tom miserable. Monday mornings always found him miserable, because Monday meant another long week of school.
Tom lay in bed, trying to think of an excuse to stay home. He checked himself for symptoms. He thought he might be getting a stomachache, but it faded before he could get his hopes up. Then he discovered something—one of his front teeth was loose! He was about to start groaning when he realized that if Aunt Polly found out, she'd just yank it out. Better save the tooth for later.
Then he remembered hearing the doctor talk about a disease that could lay a person up for weeks. Tom pulled his sore toe out from under the covers and examined it with great interest. He didn't actually know what the symptoms were, but it seemed worth a try.
"Oh!" he groaned. "Ohhhh, my sore toe!"
Sid kept sleeping.
Tom groaned louder. "Ohhhh, I'm dying!"
Nothing from Sid.
Tom shook him. "Sid! Sid!"
Sid finally woke up. "Tom? What's the matter?"
Tom moaned, "Don't jostle me. My sore toe is turning black and about to fall off."
"Tom! Let me get Aunt Polly—"
"No! It'll pass. Don't call anyone. I just want you to know—if I don't make it, Sid—I forgive you for everything. Tell everyone I said so. And give my cat and my window sash to that new girl who just moved to town..."
But Sid was already thundering down the stairs. "Aunt Polly! Come quick! Tom's dying!"
"Dying?" She flew upstairs with Sid and Mary right behind her. Her face went white. "Tom! What's wrong with you?"
"Oh, Auntie—my sore toe is infected!"
The old woman sank into a chair and laughed, then cried, then laughed again. "Tom Sawyer, you gave me a heart attack. Now stop that nonsense and get out of that bed."
The groaning stopped. The toe felt fine. "It really did seem infected, Aunt Polly. It hurt so bad I forgot all about my tooth."
"Your tooth? What's wrong with your tooth?"
"One of them's loose, and it aches something awful."
"Open your mouth. Well, your tooth is loose, all right, but you're not going to die from it. Mary, get me a silk thread and a hot coal from the kitchen."
Tom started backpedaling fast. "Please don't pull it! It doesn't hurt anymore! Please, Aunt Polly—I don't want to stay home from school!"
"Oh, so now you don't want to stay home! This whole production was about skipping school." She tied one end of the thread to his tooth and the other end to the bedpost. Then she waved the hot coal near his face—and out popped the tooth.
But there was a silver lining. On his way to school, every boy he passed was jealous of the gap in his teeth, because it let him spit in a spectacular new way. He gathered a following of admirers.
Shortly after, Tom ran into Huckleberry Finn—the most envied boy in town. Huck was the son of the town drunk. He didn't have to go to school, didn't have to go to church, didn't answer to anyone. He went fishing whenever he wanted, slept wherever he pleased, and nobody told him what to do. He wore ragged hand-me-downs from grown men, and his hat was full of holes. Every respectable mother in St. Petersburg hated him, and every boy wished he could be him.
"Hey, Huckleberry! What've you got there?"
"Dead cat."
"Lemme see. What are dead cats good for?"
"Curing warts."
The two boys fell into a long, serious discussion about warts, dead cats, spells, and superstitions—the kind of conversation that felt like the most important thing in the world. Huck was heading to the graveyard that night to try the dead cat cure, and Tom made him promise to come get him first.
Tom was late to school. He marched in briskly, hoping to slide into his seat unnoticed.
"Thomas Sawyer!"
Tom knew that whenever his full name was used, it meant trouble.
"Sir?"
"Come here. Why are you late again?"
Tom was about to lie, but then he noticed two long braids of yellow hair hanging down a back he didn't recognize. The only empty seat on the girls' side of the room was right next to those braids. He made a split-second decision.
"I stopped to talk with Huckleberry Finn!"
The teacher's jaw dropped. "You—you did what?"
"Stopped to talk with Huckleberry Finn."
There was no mistaking the words. The teacher said: "Thomas Sawyer, that is the most shocking thing I have ever heard. Take off your jacket—you will be whipped. And then you will go sit with the girls."
A ripple of laughter went through the room. Tom pretended to be embarrassed, but his heart was soaring. He took his punishment and sat down at the end of the bench next to the girl with the yellow braids.
She turned away from him with a toss of her head. But Tom slid a peach across the desk toward her. She pushed it away. He slid it back. She pushed it away again—but with less force. He slid it back once more. This time she let it stay.
Tom began drawing something on his slate, hiding it with his hand. The girl tried not to look. She failed.
"Let me see," she whispered.
Tom uncovered a lopsided drawing of a house with a curly bit of smoke coming out of the chimney.
"It's nice. Draw a man."
Tom drew a figure that could have been a man or a scarecrow. "It's beautiful," she whispered. "I wish I could draw."
"It's easy. I'll teach you. What's your name?"
"Becky Thatcher. What's yours? Oh—I know. It's Thomas Sawyer."
"That's what they call me when I'm in trouble. I'm Tom when I'm good. Call me Tom, will you?"
Tom began scrawling something else on the slate, hiding the words carefully.
"Let me see!" Becky said.
"You'll tell."
"No I won't. I promise."
"You won't tell anybody? Ever?"
"I won't ever tell anybody. Let me see!"
Tom moved his hand. On the slate it said: I LOVE YOU.
"Oh, you bad thing!" She slapped his hand—but her cheeks turned pink, and she looked pleased.
At that exact moment, the teacher's hand clamped down on Tom's ear, and he was hauled across the room back to his own seat. The whole school giggled. But Tom's heart was doing cartwheels.

4.title: THE ENGAGEMENT
4.text:
Tom could not keep his mind on his schoolwork. Every time he tried to concentrate, his thoughts drifted right back to Becky Thatcher.
At noon, he whispered in her ear: "Put on your bonnet and pretend you're going home. When you get to the corner, sneak back through the lane. I'll meet you at the school."
They met at the empty schoolhouse a few minutes later. They sat together with a slate between them, and Tom tried to teach Becky to draw a house. When they got tired of drawing, they talked.
"Were you ever at a circus?" Tom asked.
"Yes! My pa's going to take me again sometime."
"I've been to the circus lots of times. When I grow up, I'm going to be a clown."
"Oh, that would be wonderful!"
Tom paused, then said carefully, "Say, Becky—were you ever engaged?"
"What's that?"
"Engaged to be married."
"No. What's it like?"
"It's easy. You just tell someone you'll never love anybody else, ever, and then you kiss. That's all."
"Kiss? Why?"
"Because that's what people do when they're in love. Do you remember what I wrote on the slate?"
"Y-yes."
Tom leaned close and whispered in her ear: "I love you." Then he said, "Now you whisper it to me."
Becky hesitated. "Turn your face away so you can't see."
He turned. She leaned in and whispered, "I—love—you."
Then she jumped up and ran around the room, and Tom chased her, laughing. When he finally caught up, she hid her face behind her hands, and Tom said:
"Now, Becky, it's all done. After this, you're never to love anybody but me, and never to marry anybody but me, ever."
"I'll never love anybody but you, Tom. And I'll never marry anybody but you."
"Good! And we always walk together to school and from school—when nobody's looking. And at parties, you pick me and I pick you. That's how it works when you're engaged."
"It's so nice! I never heard of it before."
"Oh, it's great! When me and Amy Lawrence were engaged—"
Tom stopped. Becky's big eyes told him he'd made a terrible mistake.
"Oh, Tom! Then I'm not the first!"
Her eyes filled with tears.
"Don't cry, Becky. I don't care about her anymore."
"Yes you do! You know you do."
Tom tried to put his arm around her, but she pushed him away and faced the wall, crying. He tried again—same result. His pride flared up, and he walked out.
He stood outside for a while, glancing back at the door, hoping she'd come looking for him. She didn't. He started feeling awful. He screwed up his courage and went back in.
"Becky, I—I don't care for anybody but you."
She just sobbed louder.
Tom got out his most precious treasure—a brass knob from the top of an andiron—and held it out where she could see it. "Please, Becky. Won't you take it?"
She knocked it to the floor.
Tom walked out and kept walking—over the hills and far away. He did not come back to school that afternoon.
Becky ran to the door too late. "Tom! Come back!"
But he was gone. She sat down to cry, and when the other children came back from lunch, she had to hide her tears and get through the rest of the long, miserable afternoon alone.

5.title: THE GRAVEYARD
5.text:
That night, at half past nine, Tom and Sid were sent to bed. Sid fell asleep quickly, but Tom lay awake, restless and impatient. He stared into the darkness and waited.
Every small sound seemed huge. The clock ticking. The stairs creaking. An owl hooting somewhere in the distance. Then—a dog howled, far away, and another answered from even farther. Tom shivered.
At last, just when he thought he couldn't wait another second, he heard a faint sound outside: "Meow! Meow!"
That was the signal. Tom eased open the window, crept across the roof on all fours, and dropped to the ground. Huckleberry Finn was waiting in the shadows, holding a dead cat by the tail.
They set off through the dark streets toward the graveyard.
It was an old cemetery on a hill about a mile outside of town—a run-down place with a crooked fence and weeds growing over everything. The wooden grave markers tilted at crazy angles, and the painted words on them had faded to nothing. The boys picked their way through the tall grass by moonlight.
"Hucky," Tom whispered, "do you think the dead people mind us being here?"
"I wish I knew. It sure feels spooky."
"I bet it does."
They found the fresh grave they were looking for—a man named Hoss Williams, recently buried—and hid behind a cluster of elm trees a few feet away. Then they waited.
Time crawled. The silence was enormous.
"Huck—do you think Hoss Williams can hear us?"
"I reckon his spirit can."
"I wish I'd called him Mister Williams."
Tom was about to say something else when he froze. He grabbed Huck's arm.
"Sh! Did you hear that?"
"What?"
"There—listen!"
"Lord, Tom, they're coming. What do we do?"
"Stay still! Maybe they won't see us."
Three figures appeared in the darkness, moving through the graveyard, one of them carrying a lantern. Its light threw tiny spots across the ground.
"It's the devils for sure!" Huck whispered. "Three of them! We're done for!"
"Wait—those are people," Tom whispered back. "I know that voice. That's old Muff Potter."
"Are you sure?"
"Yes. And that other one—that's Injun Joe."
Huck nearly stopped breathing. "Injun Joe! What are they up to?"
The whispering died away as the three men reached the grave. The boys could see them clearly now. The third man was Dr. Robinson, a young doctor from town. Potter and Injun Joe had brought a wheelbarrow with shovels and rope. They began digging up the grave.
"Hurry," the doctor said quietly. "The moon could come out any minute."
The men grumbled but kept digging. After a while, they struck the coffin, pried off the lid, and hauled out the body—a horrible sight in the moonlight. They loaded it onto the wheelbarrow and tied it down.
Potter cut the extra rope with his knife and said: "Now, Doc, you owe us another five dollars, or the body stays right here."
"That's right," said Injun Joe.
"What are you talking about?" said the doctor. "I already paid you."
"Yeah, you did," said Injun Joe, stepping forward. His voice was low and dangerous. "But you did more than that. Five years ago, you threw me out of your father's kitchen when I came asking for food. You said I was up to no good. When I swore I'd get even, your father had me thrown in jail. Did you think I'd forget? I never forget."
He was in the doctor's face now, fist raised. The doctor suddenly swung and knocked Injun Joe flat on the ground. Potter shouted, "Don't hit my partner!" and grabbed the doctor. The two men wrestled, trampling the grass, grunting and struggling.
Injun Joe got to his feet, his eyes blazing. He snatched up Potter's knife from the ground. Then he crept around the fighting men, looking for an opening—quiet, patient, like a cat.
The doctor broke free from Potter's grip, grabbed a heavy wooden headboard from the grave, and swung it. It cracked against Potter's skull, and Potter dropped like a stone.
In the same instant, Injun Joe drove the knife into the doctor's chest.
The doctor gasped, staggered, and fell—half on top of Potter. The clouds covered the moon, and the two boys ran, blind with terror, through the darkness.
When the moonlight returned, Injun Joe stood over the two bodies, staring down at them. The doctor gave one last shudder and was still.
"That score is settled," Joe muttered.
Then he did something terrible. He took the bloody knife and placed it in Potter's hand. He sat down on the broken coffin and waited.
A few minutes later, Potter groaned and woke up. He saw the knife in his hand. He saw the blood. He saw the doctor lying dead beside him.
"Lord, Joe—how did this happen?"
"It's a bad business," Joe said calmly.
"What did I do? Joe, tell me honestly—did I do this?"
Joe let a long moment pass. Then he said: "You two were fighting. He hit you with that headboard and knocked you cold. You got up, grabbed the knife, and stabbed him. Then you fell down again."
"I—I don't remember any of it. It must have been the whiskey. I shouldn't have been drinking. Joe, please don't tell anyone. Promise me you won't tell."
"I won't tell. You've always been fair with me, Muff. I won't go back on you."
Potter stumbled to his feet, crying, and staggered away into the night.
Injun Joe watched him go and muttered to himself: "He was so drunk he won't remember the knife. When he notices it's missing, he'll be too scared to come back."
The graveyard was silent again. The murdered man, the open coffin, the empty grave—all of it lay under the cold moonlight while two horrified boys ran for their lives through the dark.

6.title: THE BLOOD OATH
6.text:
Tom and Huck ran until their lungs burned. They didn't stop until they reached the old tannery at the edge of town, where they burst through the door and collapsed in the shadows, gasping for breath.
When they could finally speak, Tom whispered: "Huckleberry, what do you think will happen?"
"If Dr. Robinson is dead, somebody's going to hang."
"Do you really think so?"
"I know it, Tom."
Tom was quiet for a long time. Then he said: "Who's going to tell? Us?"
"Are you crazy? What if Injun Joe didn't hang? He'd kill us for sure."
"That's what I was thinking."
"Listen, Tom—Muff Potter doesn't know what really happened. He was knocked out cold. He can't tell."
"You're right. He was too drunk to see anything."
"Tom, we have to swear—swear to keep our mouths shut."
"I'm with you. We'll swear."
Huck shook his head. "A regular swear isn't enough for something like this. There has to be writing. And blood."
Tom's whole soul thrilled at this. It was dark and dangerous and perfect. He found a flat piece of pine in the moonlight, dug a bit of red chalk from his pocket, and slowly, painfully, scratching each letter by the light of the moon, he wrote:
HUCK FINN AND TOM SAWYER
SWEAR THEY WILL KEEP MUM
ABOUT THIS AND THEY WISH
THEY MAY DROP DOWN DEAD
IF THEY EVER TELL AND ROT.
Huck was impressed. Tom unwound thread from a needle, and each boy pricked his thumb and squeezed out a drop of blood. They signed their initials—Tom in shaky letters, Huck with a wobbly H and F. They buried the wooden oath near the wall, performed some made-up rituals over it, and felt that their tongues were now truly locked. The key was thrown away forever.
A dark figure appeared at the other end of the ruined building—but the boys didn't see it.
"Tom," Huck whispered, "does this mean we can never tell? Ever?"
"Never. No matter what. We'd drop dead if we did."
They whispered a while longer, then parted ways and crept home through the dark.
Tom climbed back in through his window. He undressed as quietly as he could and fell asleep, not knowing that Sid had been lying awake the entire time, watching him.
The next morning, Tom woke to find it was late. He hurried downstairs. The family sat at the table in strange, heavy silence. Nobody scolded him. Nobody even looked at him. There were only averted eyes and a gloomy air that chilled him to the bone.
After breakfast, Aunt Polly took him aside. Tom almost felt hopeful—maybe she was going to punish him, and punishment he could handle. But she didn't punish him. She cried over him. She asked him how he could go on breaking her heart. She told him to go ahead and ruin himself and bring her gray hairs to the grave, because she just couldn't help him anymore.
This was a thousand times worse than a whipping. Tom's heart was shattered. He cried and begged for forgiveness, promised to do better—and then was sent away, feeling that he'd only half been forgiven.
He stumbled to school, miserable and distracted. He and Joe Harper both got whipped for skipping school the day before, but Tom hardly noticed. He had bigger things on his mind.
He slumped into his seat and put his head in his hands. After a while, his elbow touched something. He picked it up—a little brass knob. His andiron knob. The one Becky had knocked to the floor.
His heart broke all over again.

7.title: THE TOWN IN SHOCK
7.text:
By noon, the whole village knew. No one needed a telegraph—the news flew from mouth to mouth like wildfire. Dr. Robinson was dead. A bloody knife had been found near the body, and someone had recognized it as Muff Potter's. A man had spotted Potter washing himself in a stream at two in the morning, and Potter had run away when he saw he'd been noticed.
Horsemen were sent out in every direction. The sheriff was confident Potter would be caught before nightfall.
The whole town drifted toward the graveyard. Tom went too—not because he wanted to, but because something he couldn't explain dragged him there. He pushed through the crowd and saw the terrible scene: the open grave, the trampled grass, the bloodstains.
Someone pinched his arm. He turned—it was Huck. Their eyes met, and both looked away instantly, terrified that someone might notice.
All around them, people were talking: "Poor young fellow." "This should be a lesson." "Muff Potter will hang for sure."
Tom shivered—because across the crowd, standing calm and still, was Injun Joe.
Just then, a commotion rippled through the gathering. "It's him! He's coming!"
"Who?"
"Muff Potter!"
Potter stumbled into the clearing, looking dazed and terrified. He wasn't trying to escape—he just seemed confused. He stopped in front of the body, and his whole body began to shake. He covered his face and burst into tears.
"I didn't do it, friends. I swear I didn't."
"Nobody's accused you," someone called out—but the words hit Potter like a slap.
He looked around desperately, and his eyes landed on Injun Joe. "Oh, Joe—you promised you wouldn't—"
The sheriff pushed through the crowd. "Is this your knife?" He held it up.
Potter would have collapsed if people hadn't caught him. He sank to the ground and moaned, "Something told me I should've come back for that knife, but I was afraid..."
Tom and Huck stood frozen, watching the stone-faced liar tell his story to the sheriff, calm as a summer morning. They expected lightning to strike him dead at any moment. But it didn't. The sky stayed clear, and Injun Joe walked away free.
The boys made a new silent vow: they would never, ever open their mouths.
In the days that followed, Tom's secret ate at him like acid. He talked in his sleep, which nearly gave everything away. One morning at breakfast, Sid said: "Tom, you toss and talk so much at night you keep me awake. Last night you kept saying, 'It's blood, it's blood, that's what it is!' and 'Don't torment me—I'll tell!'"
Tom went pale. Luckily, Aunt Polly said, "That dreadful murder—I dream about it nearly every night myself." And the moment passed.
But Tom began tying a bandage around his jaw at night, pretending he had a toothache, so that if he talked in his sleep, the words would be muffled. He never knew that Sid would sometimes untie the bandage in the dark and listen.
Every day, Tom watched for a chance to visit the jail. It was a small brick building at the edge of a swamp—no guards, barely locked. Tom and Huck would sneak up to the grated window and pass through small gifts—tobacco, matches, whatever they could find. Potter's gratitude was heartbreaking.
"You boys are the only friends I've got left," Potter would say, his voice cracking. "I used to mend kites for all the boys, and show them the good fishing spots. Now they've all forgotten me—except you two. I did an awful thing, boys. Drunk and crazy, that's the only way I can account for it. And now I've got to swing for it."
Tom went home sick with guilt every time.

8.title: PIRATES OF JACKSON'S ISLAND
8.text:
Tom's mind was made up. He was a friendless, miserable boy. Nobody loved him. Aunt Polly was disappointed in him. Becky Thatcher wouldn't speak to him. The secret of the murder was crushing his soul. Fine. If this was how the world wanted to treat him, he would leave. He would run away and never come back. Then they'd all be sorry.
He was walking down Meadow Lane, feeling very dramatic about it all, when he ran into Joe Harper. Joe looked just as grim.
It turned out Joe was running away too. His mother had whipped him for drinking cream that he'd never even touched—she'd thrown it out herself and forgotten. So Joe figured she was tired of him and wanted him gone.
The two miserable boys walked together, swearing a solemn pact to stand by each other forever. Joe wanted to be a hermit and live in a cave, but Tom convinced him that being a pirate was much better.
Three miles below St. Petersburg, in the middle of the wide Mississippi, lay Jackson's Island—a long, narrow strip of wooded land. Nobody lived there. It was perfect.
They found Huck Finn and recruited him. He didn't care what they did—any career was fine with him. They agreed to meet at midnight on the riverbank, two miles above town, where a small log raft was tied up.
At midnight, Tom arrived with a stolen ham and a few other supplies. The night was still and full of stars. The great river lay wide and dark, like a sleeping ocean.
Tom gave a low whistle. It was answered from below the bluff. He whistled twice more.
"Who goes there?" came a voice.
"Tom Sawyer, the Black Avenger of the Spanish Main. Name yourselves."
"Huck Finn the Red-Handed. Joe Harper, the Terror of the Seas."
"Give the password."
Two voices whispered the same word at the same time: "Blood!"
Tom tumbled his ham over the bluff and climbed down after it. Joe had brought a side of bacon and some corn bread. Huck brought a skillet, some tobacco, and a few corn cobs for making pipes.
They shoved off on the raft, Tom at the helm, giving whispered orders in proper pirate fashion: "Luff and bring her to the wind!" "Steady, steady!" "Let her go off a point!" None of these commands meant anything in particular, but they sounded magnificent.
The raft drifted past the sleeping town. Two or three faint lights glimmered in the distance. Tom stood with his arms folded, taking one last dramatic look at the place he was leaving forever—or at least until next week.
Around two in the morning, the raft grounded on a sandbar at the head of Jackson's Island. The boys unloaded their supplies, built a fire against a great fallen log, and cooked bacon in the skillet. They had never tasted anything so delicious. They lay on the grass afterward, staring at the flames, feeling like kings.
"Isn't this great?" said Joe.
"It's perfect," said Tom. "What would the boys at school say if they could see us?"
"They'd die of jealousy," said Huck. "I don't ever want to go back. I never get enough to eat at home, and nobody bothers me here."
"Pirates don't have to get up in the morning," said Tom. "They don't have to go to school, or wash, or any of that."
They talked about pirate life for a while—treasure and ships and sword fights. Then, one by one, they fell quiet. The fire crackled. The river murmured. And the three pirates, who had sworn to never return to civilization, began to think—just a tiny bit—about home.
They pushed those thoughts down and fell asleep under the stars.

9.title: LIFE ON THE ISLAND
9.text:
When Tom woke in the morning, he couldn't remember where he was. He sat up and looked around. A cool gray dawn filtered through the trees. The forest was perfectly still. Dew sparkled on every leaf and blade of grass. A thin ribbon of smoke rose straight up from the dying campfire.
Then the woods began to wake up. A bird called. Another answered. A woodpecker hammered somewhere in the distance. A little green worm crawled onto Tom's leg, and he sat stone-still, watching it, because if a measuring worm walks all the way across you, it means you're going to get new clothes.
Gradually the forest came alive—ants marching, a catbird singing, a blue jay flashing through the trees, a squirrel chattering at the boys from a branch. The sun broke through the canopy like golden spears.
Tom shook the other two pirates awake, and within minutes they were all stripped down and splashing in the shallow water of the sandbar, laughing and ducking each other. No one missed the little village sleeping in the distance across the wide water.
They came back to camp starving and got the fire roaring again. Huck found a cold spring nearby. Tom and Joe went to the riverbank with hooks and lines, and within minutes they had enough bass and catfish for a feast. They fried the fish with bacon and declared it the best breakfast in history.
After the meal, they explored the island. It was about three miles long and a quarter mile wide, separated from the far shore by a narrow channel. They swam every hour or so, and by afternoon they knew the island like the back of their hands.
But as the afternoon wore on and the excitement faded, a strange heaviness crept over them. They stopped talking. They stopped playing. They sat looking across the water at the distant town and thought about things they didn't want to think about.
Suddenly, a deep, dull boom echoed across the river.
"What was that?" Joe whispered.
"I don't know," said Tom.
"It isn't thunder," said Huck.
They crept to the shore and peered through the bushes. The town's small ferryboat was in the river about a mile below the village. People crowded its deck. Then a great puff of white smoke burst from the boat's side, and after a pause, the same heavy boom rolled across the water.
"I know!" Tom cried. "Somebody's drowned! They shoot a cannon over the water—it's supposed to make the body come up."
"That's right," said Huck. "They did the same thing last summer when Bill Turner drowned."
"I wonder who it is," said Joe.
They watched the boat go back and forth. Then, slowly, it dawned on Tom.
"Boys," he said, "I know who drowned. It's us!"
They felt like heroes. They were missed! They were mourned! People were crying over them! They were the talk of the whole town! It was glorious.
As night fell, the ferryboat gave up and went home. The pirates returned to camp, puffed up with pride. They caught more fish, cooked supper, and spent the evening imagining what the whole village must be saying about them.
But when the fire burned low and the darkness closed in, the pride faded. Tom and Joe couldn't stop thinking about certain people at home who were not having a good time right now. They grew quiet and miserable.
Joe timidly suggested that maybe they could go home—not right now, but maybe—
Tom shut him down instantly. "Go home? A pirate doesn't go home! We'd be laughed at!"
But later that night, after Joe and Huck had fallen asleep, Tom lay awake thinking. Carefully, quietly, he wrote something on two pieces of sycamore bark. He put one in his pocket and one in Joe's hat. Then he crept out of camp and disappeared into the night.

10.title: THEIR OWN FUNERAL
10.text:
Tom waded across the shallow sandbar, swam the deeper channel, and made his way through the dark woods to the riverbank opposite town. He slipped into the water again, swam to the ferryboat landing, and sneaked aboard a small rowboat tied to the stern. When the last ferry of the night crossed, Tom rode along underneath.
He crept through quiet streets to his aunt's house. A light was burning in the sitting room. He pressed himself against the door, lifted the latch, and eased the door open—just a crack.
Aunt Polly, Sid, Mary, and Joe Harper's mother sat together in the lamplight. They had been crying. They were talking about the boys.
"He wasn't a bad boy," Aunt Polly was saying, her voice breaking. "Only mischievous. Just high-spirited, that's all. He never meant any harm, and he was the kindest-hearted boy in the world." She started crying again.
"It was just the same with my Joe," Mrs. Harper sobbed. "Full of mischief, but as sweet and generous as could be. And to think I whipped him for drinking that cream, when I was the one who threw it out! I'll never see him again."
"The Lord giveth and the Lord taketh away," Aunt Polly said. "But it's so hard. Only last Saturday, Tom filled the cat full of that terrible medicine, and I smacked him—and now I wish I could do it all over. I'd hug him instead."
Tom was sniffling under the bed by now, feeling so sorry for himself—and for his aunt—that he could barely stand it. He learned that the funeral would be preached on Sunday morning, since no bodies had been found. The whole town assumed the boys had drowned while rafting across the river.
After Mrs. Harper left, and Aunt Polly finally went to bed, Tom crept out. He stood over her sleeping form for a long moment. He took the piece of sycamore bark from his pocket and started to place it beside her candle—then stopped. A better idea had struck him. A magnificent idea.
He put the bark back in his pocket, bent down and kissed his aunt's forehead, and slipped out into the night.
He made it back to Jackson's Island by dawn. He found his friends still sleeping and collapsed beside them. When they woke, he told them everything—well, almost everything. He told them the town thought they were dead. He told them about the funeral. And then he told them his idea.
Their eyes went wide.
"Tom Sawyer," said Joe, "that is the greatest idea I have ever heard."
Sunday morning came. The church bell tolled slowly—not its usual bright ring, but a single, mournful note. The villagers gathered in silence, dressed in black. The little church had never been so full.
Aunt Polly entered, followed by Sid and Mary. Mrs. Harper came next with her family. They were led to the front pew while the whole congregation stood in respect.
The minister spoke about the lost boys—their good hearts, their sweet natures, their playful spirits. He told stories about them that made people laugh through their tears. By the time he was done, every person in the church was weeping openly. Even the minister himself was crying.
And then—a creak.
The church door opened, just a little. Every head turned.
Three boys came marching up the aisle: Tom in the lead, Joe next, and Huck bringing up the rear in his usual collection of rags, looking like he'd rather be anywhere else on earth.
They had been hiding in the church gallery the whole time, listening to their own funeral.
Aunt Polly, Mary, and Mrs. Harper threw themselves on the boys, smothering them with hugs and kisses and tears. Huck stood off to the side, uncomfortable and embarrassed, until Tom said: "Aunt Polly, somebody ought to be glad to see Huck, too."
"And so I am!" she cried, and wrapped him up in a hug that made him squirm.
The minister shouted: "Praise God! Now—sing! And put your hearts in it!"
And they did. The old hymn rang out so strong it shook the rafters, and Tom Sawyer the Pirate looked around at the faces of every kid in town—all of them green with envy—and decided that this was the proudest moment of his entire life.

11.title: TOM'S DREAM TRICK
11.text:
At breakfast on Monday, Aunt Polly was showering Tom with love, but underneath it, something was bothering her.
"Well, Tom," she finally said, "I don't say it wasn't a fine joke to keep everybody worrying for a whole week while you boys had your fun. But it is a shame you could be so hard-hearted. If you could float across on a log to attend your own funeral, you could have floated across to let me know you weren't dead."
"You could have done that, Tom," said Mary. "I believe you would have, if you'd thought of it."
"Would you, Tom?" Aunt Polly's eyes were hopeful. "Would you have, if you'd thought of it?"
"I—well, I don't know. It would have spoiled everything."
Aunt Polly's face fell. "Tom, I hoped you loved me enough to think of it. It would have been something if you'd even cared enough to consider it."
"Now, Auntie, it's not that bad," Mary said. "That's just Tom's way—he's always in such a rush he never thinks ahead."
"I wish I had thought of it," Tom said, and he meant it. "But I dreamed about you. That's something."
"A cat does that much. But it's better than nothing. What did you dream?"
Tom had to think fast. "Well—Wednesday night I dreamed you were sitting right over there by the bed. And Sid was by the woodbox, and Mary next to him."
"Well, so we were! That's where we always sit. Go on."
"And I dreamed that Joe Harper's mother was here."
"She was! Did you dream anything else?"
"Oh, lots. But it's kind of fuzzy—"
"Try harder, Tom."
"It seems like—the wind blew the—the—"
"What? What did the wind blow?"
"It blew the candle!"
"Mercy! Go on!"
"And it seems like you said the door was open—"
"As sure as I'm sitting here, I did! Mary, didn't I?"
"And then you made Sid go shut it."
"Well, I never! Go on, Tom!"
"Then you said I wasn't bad, just mischievous, and not any more responsible than—a colt, or something."
"That's exactly what I said! Goodness gracious, go on!"
"And then you started crying."
"So I did."
"And then Mrs. Harper cried too, and said she wished she hadn't whipped Joe for the cream—"
"Tom! It's like you were right there in the room! What else?"
Tom rolled out every detail he could remember from hiding under the bed. Aunt Polly's eyes grew wider with every word. By the end she was fully convinced it had been a genuine prophetic dream—maybe even sent by angels.
"And then I thought you prayed for me," Tom said quietly. "And I could hear every word. And you went to bed, and I was so sorry—I took a piece of sycamore bark and wrote on it, 'We aren't dead—we're only off being pirates.' And I put it on the table by the candle. And then you looked so peaceful, sleeping there, that I bent down and kissed you."
"Did you, Tom? Did you really?"
"Yes ma'am."
"I forgive you everything for that!" She crushed him in a hug. "Even though it was only a dream."
"Shut up, Sid," Tom muttered, catching his brother's skeptical look.
After Tom left for school, Aunt Polly's heart struggled between joy and suspicion. She went to the closet and got out the ragged jacket Tom had worn on his pirate adventure. She held it, arguing with herself: "I won't look. It's a good lie—a kind lie. I won't let it grieve me."
But she looked. She reached into the pocket—and pulled out a piece of sycamore bark.
She read it through tears that wouldn't stop falling. It said: WE AIN'T DEAD—WE ARE ONLY OFF BEING PIRATES.
"I could forgive that boy," she whispered, "if he'd committed a million sins."

12.title: MAKING UP WITH BECKY
12.text:
Something about Aunt Polly's kiss that morning lifted all the weight off Tom's shoulders. He felt light and happy for the first time in weeks. On his way to school, he ran into Becky Thatcher.
"Becky, I acted awful mean the other day, and I'm so sorry. I won't ever do it again. Please—can we make up?"
She stopped and gave him a cold stare. "I'll thank you to keep away from me, Tom Sawyer. I'm never speaking to you again."
She tossed her braids and walked on.
Tom was so stunned he couldn't even think of a comeback. His good mood evaporated. He stomped into the schoolyard, furious.
For the rest of the morning, Tom noticed Becky laughing and running around the yard, always managing to end up close to him, clearly trying to get his attention. He pretended not to notice. When he saw her looking his way, he turned and talked loudly to Amy Lawrence instead.
Becky's smile faded. She tried to look cheerful, but the light went out of her face. She drifted away.
Meanwhile, something dangerous was happening. The teacher, Mr. Dobbins, kept a mysterious book in his desk—locked up, always hidden. Every kid in school was dying to know what it was. On this particular day, as Becky passed the teacher's desk, she noticed the key was in the lock.
She glanced around. The room was empty. She turned the key and opened the book. The title page said Professor Somebody's Anatomy. She didn't know what that meant, so she started flipping through it—and came upon a picture of a human body, completely naked.
At that exact moment, Tom walked through the door. Becky slammed the book shut in a panic—and tore the picture right down the middle. She shoved the book back in the desk, turned the key, and burst into tears.
"Tom Sawyer! You are the meanest person alive, sneaking up on people!"
"How was I supposed to know you were looking at anything?"
"You're going to tell on me, and I'll get whipped, and I've never been whipped in school!"
She stormed out, crying.
Tom stood there, bewildered. "What a fuss over nothing," he thought. "Girls are so thin-skinned. I'm not going to tell old Dobbins. But he'll figure it out on his own. He'll ask every kid in the room, and when he gets to Becky, she won't be able to hide it—girls can never keep a straight face. She's going to get it. Too bad for her."
He paused. "But she would have let ME get in trouble without thinking twice. All right, let her sweat."
But he didn't feel right about it.
That afternoon, Mr. Dobbins took the book from his desk. He settled into his chair to read it—and found the torn page.
Every student sat up straight. Tom glanced at Becky. She looked like a trapped rabbit.
Mr. Dobbins stood and faced the room. "Who tore this book?"
Dead silence. You could hear a pin drop.
"Benjamin Rogers, did you tear this book?"
"No sir."
"Joseph Harper, did you?"
"No sir."
One by one, the teacher worked his way through the class. Tom's heart pounded harder with each name. The teacher turned to the girls.
"Amy Lawrence?"
She shook her head.
"Susan Harper?"
No.
"Rebecca Thatcher."
Becky's face went white. Tom looked at her. Her eyes were wide with terror, her hands pressed together.
"Did you tear this book?"
Her lips moved, but no sound came out.
Tom leaped to his feet. "I did it!"
The whole school stared in shock.
Mr. Dobbins said nothing for a long, terrible moment. Then: "Come here."
Tom walked forward. He took the worst whipping Mr. Dobbins had ever given, plus two hours of detention after school. He didn't make a sound.
And the whole time, he could feel Becky Thatcher's eyes on him—shining with gratitude and something that looked an awful lot like love.
Tom went to bed that night still stinging from the whipping, but Becky's last words kept playing in his head like music:
"Tom, how could you be so noble!"

13.title: EXAMINATION DAY
13.text:
Vacation was coming, and Mr. Dobbins was getting meaner by the day. He seemed to take a vicious pleasure in whipping students for the smallest mistakes. The younger kids spent their days in terror and their nights plotting revenge.
They finally came up with a plan—and it was a beauty.
The sign-painter's boy was in on it. His father rented a room to Mr. Dobbins, so the boy had his own reasons for wanting revenge. The plan required the teacher's wife to be out of town, and it required Mr. Dobbins to be drunk—neither of which was unusual.
On Examination Evening, the schoolhouse was packed. Parents, town leaders, and visitors filled every seat. The teacher sat on a raised platform with a blackboard behind him, looking only slightly unsteady. Rows of students sat on a temporary stage—boys scrubbed raw and miserable in stiff clothes, girls in white dresses with ribbons in their hair.
The performances began. A tiny boy recited a poem with robotic gestures and nearly fainted from stage fright. A girl lisped her way through "Mary Had a Little Lamb." Tom Sawyer stepped forward with grand confidence and launched into a dramatic speech—"Give me liberty or give me death!"—but froze halfway through. Stage fright hit him like a wall. His legs shook, his throat closed. The audience stared in painful silence. He gave up and slunk off the stage.
More recitations followed. Then came the spelling bee, the Latin class, and finally the main event—original essays by the older girls. Every single essay, no matter the topic, ended with the same kind of preachy moral lesson. The audience clapped politely and pretended to be moved.
Then Mr. Dobbins stood to lead the geography class. He turned to the blackboard and began drawing a map of America. But his hand was unsteady from drink, and the map came out crooked and wrong. A ripple of laughter ran through the crowd. He erased and tried again—worse. The laughter grew louder.
He concentrated harder, determined to get it right. He didn't notice what was happening above him.
In the ceiling, there was a small opening—a trapdoor to the attic. Through it, slowly, came a cat. She was tied to a rope and lowered inch by inch, a rag around her head to keep her quiet. She dangled and twisted, clawing at the air, getting closer and closer to the teacher's head.
The audience was shaking with laughter now. The cat was six inches from Mr. Dobbins—four inches—two—
She grabbed his wig with both paws and was yanked back up into the attic, taking the wig with her.
The teacher's bald head gleamed in the lamplight. The sign-painter's boy had gilded it while the teacher napped. It shone like a mirror.
The room exploded. The meeting was over. The boys were avenged. And vacation had begun.

14.title: A MISERABLE SUMMER
14.text:
Tom joined the Cadets of Temperance, attracted by their fancy uniforms. He promised to stop swearing and smoking. Naturally, the moment he promised not to do these things, they were all he could think about. He quit after two days.
The Fourth of July was a disappointment—it rained, and a visiting senator turned out to be a normal-sized man, which was deeply unsatisfying.
A circus came to town. The boys played circus in the backyard for three days and then got bored.
Becky Thatcher left for the summer to visit relatives in another town. Without her, nothing had any flavor.
And underneath it all, like a toothache that never stopped, was the murder. The secret pressed on Tom's conscience every single day.
Then came the measles. Tom was bedridden for two weeks, completely cut off from the world. When he finally dragged himself outside, everything had changed. A religious revival had swept through St. Petersburg. Everyone had "gotten religion"—even the boys. Tom went looking for a friendly face and found Joe Harper reading the Bible. Ben Rogers was delivering charity baskets. Jim Hollis told Tom his measles were God's warning to shape up. Even Huckleberry Finn greeted him with a Bible verse.
Tom's heart sank. He went home and crawled into bed, convinced he was the only lost soul left in town.
That night, a terrible thunderstorm struck. Lightning split the sky, thunder shook the house, and rain lashed the windows. Tom pulled the covers over his head, certain that all of this was aimed directly at him—that God had finally had enough of Tom Sawyer.
But the storm passed, and he was still alive.
Then the measles came back. He spent three more weeks in bed. When he finally recovered, the revival was over. Joe Harper was back to mischief. Ben Rogers had gone back to normal. Huck was eating a stolen melon behind a fence.
Everything was just as it had been—except that the murder trial was about to begin.

15.title: THE TRIAL
15.text:
The trial of Muff Potter consumed the entire village. It was all anyone talked about. Tom couldn't escape it—every conversation, every rumor, every whispered opinion was like a needle in his conscience. He pulled Huck to a private spot to make sure they were still in agreement.
"Huck, have you told anybody?"
"Told anybody about what?"
"You know what."
"Of course I haven't. Not one word. Why?"
"I was just making sure. I'm scared, Huck."
"Me too. If Injun Joe ever found out—we wouldn't last two days."
They swore their oath again, just to be safe.
The trial dragged on for days. Witness after witness testified against Muff Potter. They described finding the knife, seeing Potter wash blood off himself in the middle of the night, his suspicious behavior. Potter's own lawyer barely asked a single question. The courtroom grew restless—did the defense attorney plan to just give up?
Tom and Huck haunted the edges of the courthouse, drawn by the same horrible fascination. Every day, they heard worse news. The evidence was piling up. The verdict seemed certain.
On the last day of the trial, the courtroom was packed. Potter sat in chains, pale and broken, his eyes empty. Injun Joe sat nearby, calm and expressionless as stone.
The prosecution rested its case. Potter's lawyer stood and said something that shocked the room: "Call Thomas Sawyer to the stand."
Every eye turned to Tom as he walked forward. He looked terrified. He was terrified. He took the oath and sat down.
"Thomas Sawyer, where were you on the night of June seventeenth, around midnight?"
Tom looked at Injun Joe's face—hard, dark, and still as iron. His tongue wouldn't work. The audience leaned forward. Silence stretched like a wire about to snap.
Finally, Tom managed to push words out, barely above a whisper: "In the graveyard."
"A little louder, please. Where were you?"
"In the graveyard."
Injun Joe's eyes flickered—just slightly.
"Were you near the grave of Hoss Williams?"
"Yes, sir."
"How near?"
"As near as I am to you right now."
"Were you hidden?"
"Yes, sir. Behind the elm trees."
"Was anyone with you?"
"Yes, sir."
"Tell us everything that happened. Tell it in your own way. Don't skip anything."
Tom began slowly, haltingly. But as the story poured out of him, his voice grew stronger. Every person in that courtroom stopped breathing. They sat frozen, mouths open, eyes fixed on this boy who was describing, in vivid and terrible detail, everything that had happened in the graveyard that night.
"—and as the doctor swung the headboard and hit Muff Potter, Injun Joe jumped up with the knife and—"
CRASH!
Injun Joe hurled himself at the nearest window. Glass exploded outward. Before anyone could move, he was through it and gone—running, leaping over a fence, disappearing into the night.
The courtroom erupted into chaos. But Muff Potter was free.
And Tom Sawyer was a hero—again.

16.title: TREASURE HUNTING
16.text:
Tom was the golden boy of St. Petersburg once more. His name appeared in the local paper. People said he might be president someday.
But at night, the dreams came. Injun Joe was in every one of them—always lurking, always watching, always with murder in his eyes. Huck was just as frightened. Tom had told the lawyer everything, which meant Huck's share in the secret might eventually come out.
A detective came from St. Louis, poked around town, shook his head wisely, and went home without finding anything useful. Injun Joe had vanished.
Slowly, day by day, Tom's fear faded. He would never feel completely safe until Injun Joe was caught or confirmed dead. But boys' minds don't stay in one place for long, and before he knew it, Tom was consumed by a new obsession: buried treasure.
He found Huck and presented the idea.
"Where do we dig?" Huck asked.
"That's the thing—treasure is always buried in specific places. Under dead trees, in haunted houses, on islands."
"Who buries it?"
"Robbers, obviously."
"If I had treasure, I'd spend it."
"So would I. But robbers always hide it and then forget where they put it. Or they die. Then somebody finds an old yellow map with mysterious markings, and after about a week of working out the clues, they find the treasure."
Huck was sold. They grabbed a pick and a shovel and hiked three miles to a dead tree on a hillside. They dug for hours. Nothing. They tried a different spot. Nothing. They tried yet another.
"Do they always bury it this deep?" Huck asked, wiping sweat from his face.
"Not always. I think we've got the wrong place."
They tried two more spots before Tom slapped his forehead. "Wait—you have to find where the shadow of the dead branch falls at midnight, and that's where you dig!"
"Then we've wasted the whole day! We have to come back at night."
They hid their tools and came back that night by moonlight. It was eerie work—the wind moaning through the trees, the darkness pressing in. They dug where the shadow fell. Still nothing.
"I know what we should do," Tom said at last. "We should try the haunted house."
Huck's face went pale. "Tom, I don't like haunted houses. They're worse than graveyards. Dead people might talk to you, but ghosts sneak up behind you and breathe on your neck."
"Ghosts only come out at night. We'd go in the daytime."
Huck thought it over. "All right. But I don't like it."

17.title: THE HAUNTED HOUSE
17.text:
On Saturday, the boys hiked to the old abandoned house on Still-House Branch. The place was a wreck—fences gone, weeds everywhere, chimney crumbling, windows empty and dark. They crept inside, hearts hammering, speaking in whispers.
The ground floor was a ruin: floorboards rotting, fireplace cold, cobwebs hanging in thick curtains from every beam. They explored carefully, jumping at every creak. After a while, their courage grew. They even climbed the rickety stairs to the second floor, where they found nothing but an empty closet and more decay.
They were about to head back down to start digging when Tom grabbed Huck's arm.
"Sh! Hear that?"
"Yes! Let's run!"
"Stay still—they're coming through the door—"
The boys dropped flat on their bellies and pressed their eyes to cracks in the floorboards. Two men walked in below them. One was a ragged, mean-looking stranger. The other appeared to be an old, deaf Spaniard who'd been spotted around town a few times—dark skin, white whiskers, green glasses, a wide-brimmed hat.
But when the "Spaniard" spoke, the boys nearly choked with terror. The voice belonged to Injun Joe.
"No," said the ragged man, "I've thought it over, and I don't like it. It's too dangerous."
"Dangerous?" Joe growled. "Don't be a coward."
They talked about some kind of "job" they had planned—something risky that involved another town upriver. Then Joe said: "We'll do that job after I've had one more look around this town. Then we head for Texas."
The men ate lunch. Then, to the boys' combined horror and fascination, Joe said: "We might as well bury the money while we're here—bury it deep."
His partner went to the far wall, knelt down, lifted a hearthstone, and pulled out a bag that clinked heavily. He counted out some coins for each of them and handed the rest to Joe, who was already on his knees in the corner, digging with his bowie knife.
The boys above could barely contain themselves. Six hundred and fifty dollars in silver! Right there!
Joe's knife struck something solid.
"What's that?" said the stranger.
"Half-rotten plank—no, it's a box." Joe broke through the wood and reached inside. "It's money!"
He pulled out a handful of gold coins. The men stared at them, then at each other.
"There's thousands here," Joe whispered. "This must be from Murrel's gang—they used to operate around here."
"Now we don't need to do that other job."
Joe's face darkened. "You don't know me. That job isn't about money. It's about revenge. I'll need your help. After it's done—then Texas."
"All right. What do we do with all this gold? Bury it again?"
"Yes." The boys above nearly burst with joy. "No—wait." Their hearts dropped. "That pick and shovel over there—they've got fresh dirt on them. Who brought them here? Who's been digging?"
Ice ran through Tom's veins. Joe was staring at the boys' tools, which they'd left in the corner.
"We'll take the money to my other hideout," Joe said. "Number Two—under the cross."
"Which one?"
"Not Number One—too risky. Number Two."
Joe stood and moved from window to window, peering out. "Whoever brought those tools could be upstairs right now."
The boys stopped breathing. Joe put his hand on his knife and started for the staircase. The boys were about to dive for the closet when—CRACK—the rotten stairs collapsed under Joe's weight. He crashed to the ground floor in a shower of splintered wood.
His partner said: "Forget it. If anyone's up there, let them stay. It'll be dark soon—let them follow us if they dare."
Joe cursed and grumbled, but agreed. The two men gathered the treasure box and slipped out into the fading light.
Tom and Huck lay on the floor for a long, long time before they dared to move. When they finally crept down and out of the house, they were shaking.
"Revenge," Tom whispered. "Who do you think he means?"
"I don't know, but—Tom, what if he means us?"
They walked home in grim silence, sick with the knowledge that Injun Joe was somewhere close, with a knife and a grudge and a box full of gold.

18.title: TRACKING NUMBER TWO
18.text:
The treasure haunted Tom's dreams—but so did Injun Joe. The gold was out there somewhere, hidden in a place called "Number Two, under the cross." Tom had to find it. But he also had to avoid getting killed.
He and Huck talked it through. "Number Two" had to be a room number—probably in one of the town's two taverns. Tom went to investigate. At the better tavern, Room Number Two was occupied by a young lawyer. At the cheaper tavern, the owner's son said Room Number Two was always locked. Nobody went in or out except late at night. He didn't know why. Most people figured it was haunted.
"That's our place," Tom told Huck. "The back door of that room opens into the alley. You get every old key you can find, and I'll get Aunt Polly's keys. The first dark night, we'll try them all."
"What if we run into Injun Joe?"
"It'll be dark—he probably won't see us. And if he does—well, we run."
"I don't like this one bit, Tom."
"I don't love it either. But think about that gold."
They tried on Thursday night. The alley behind the tavern was pitch dark—perfect. Tom crept up to the door, heart slamming against his ribs. He tried one key. It didn't turn. He tried another—a third—
Then his hand closed on the doorknob, and the door swung open. It wasn't even locked.
He eased inside, peeled back the towel he'd wrapped around his lantern, and—
Something moved on the floor. A body. An arm. Tom's light fell on a face—Injun Joe, passed out drunk, sprawled on the floor in a puddle of whiskey.
Tom didn't breathe. He didn't think. He ran.
He burst out of the alley like a bullet and didn't stop until he found Huck three blocks away.
"Run!" he gasped. "Run!"
They didn't stop until they reached an abandoned shed on the edge of town. Rain began hammering the roof as they crouched inside, panting.
"Tom, what happened?"
"He's in there. Injun Joe. Asleep on the floor—drunk."
"Did you see the box?"
"I didn't wait to look around! I saw him and I ran."
They agreed to keep watch on the tavern. Every night, Huck would patrol the area. If he ever saw Injun Joe leave, he would come straight to Tom's window and signal. Then they'd make their move.
Huck took the first watch that night. And the next night. And the next. But Injun Joe never appeared.

19.title: THE PICNIC
19.text:
On Friday morning, Tom got the best possible news: Becky Thatcher's family had returned to town. Even better, Becky's mother had agreed to let her have the long-promised picnic the very next day.
Tom was ecstatic. By Saturday morning, a rowdy, laughing crowd of children gathered at Judge Thatcher's house and paraded through town to the ferry landing. The old steamboat carried them three miles downriver to a wooded hollow at the base of a steep, rocky hill.
The kids scattered into the woods—chasing each other, eating from picnic baskets, playing in the creek. By late afternoon, someone shouted: "Who's ready for the cave?"
Everyone was. They grabbed candles and scrambled up the hillside to McDougal's Cave.
The entrance was shaped like the letter A—a great dark mouth in the rock. Inside, the air was cold and damp. The main passage was narrow, with high limestone walls that rose sixty feet overhead. Every few yards, smaller corridors branched off in every direction. The cave was a labyrinth—miles and miles of twisting, intersecting tunnels that led nowhere and everywhere. People said you could wander for days and never find the end.
Most of the kids knew the first section of the cave and didn't venture beyond it. Groups chased each other through the dim passages, screaming and laughing, their candles throwing wild shadows on the walls. Tom and Becky ran with the rest, ducking through narrow openings, hiding in crevices, trying to scare each other.
Eventually they wandered away from the main group. They found themselves in a part of the cave they'd never seen before—no names scratched in the walls, no candle-smoke graffiti. Just raw, untouched stone.
Tom was thrilled. He wanted to explore. They followed the passage deeper and deeper, stopping to admire a cavern full of glittering stalactites, a crystal-clear underground spring, a vast room with pillars of stone where the ceiling dripped with thousands of sleeping bats.
The bats were a problem. When the candlelight disturbed them, they came shrieking down in swarms. Tom grabbed Becky's hand and they ran, ducking into side passages to escape. When the bats were finally gone, they stopped to catch their breath.
"Tom," Becky said, "I haven't heard the others in a long time."
"Come to think of it—neither have I. We're pretty far down from them."
"How long have we been walking?"
"I don't know. We should probably start heading back."
"Can you find the way?"
"Sure. I think so."
But after ten minutes of walking, nothing looked familiar. After twenty minutes, Tom wasn't talking as cheerfully. After thirty minutes, Becky noticed.
"Tom, you didn't make any marks, did you?"
His silence was answer enough.
"We're lost," Becky whispered.
"No—I'll figure it out. Come on."
But he couldn't figure it out. The passages all looked the same. He tried one direction, then another, then another. Nothing.
Becky started to cry. "We're lost, Tom! We're never going to get out!"
Tom felt cold terror creeping up his spine. But he put his arm around her and said: "We'll find the way. Don't give up."
They kept walking.

20.title: HUCK SAVES THE WIDOW
20.text:
While Tom and Becky wandered deeper into the cave, Huck Finn was keeping watch outside the tavern.
The night was cloudy and still. By ten o'clock, the town had gone to sleep. By eleven, the tavern lights went dark. Huck waited in the shadows, patient and alert. Midnight came and went. He was about to give up when—
The alley door clicked open.
Two figures slipped out. One carried something bulky under his arm. The treasure box! Huck's pulse hammered. He couldn't go get Tom now—they'd lose the men forever. He had to follow them alone.
He crept after them through the dark streets, bare feet silent on the dirt. The men turned up the cross-street, climbed the path toward Cardiff Hill, passed the old Welshman's house, and kept climbing. Huck stayed close, hidden in the shadows.
They stopped at the top of the hill, near the path that led to the Widow Douglas's house. Huck crouched behind a boulder, straining to hear.
"There are lights on," said the stranger. "She's got company."
Then Injun Joe spoke, and what he said turned Huck's blood to ice.
"Her husband was the justice who had me horsewhipped in front of the whole town, like a dog. He's dead now, so I can't get him. But I'll take it out on her."
"Don't kill her, Joe."
"I'm not going to kill her. I'm going to hurt her. Cut her face so she'll remember."
Huck had heard enough. He turned, crept backward with agonizing care—one step, then another, then another—and then he ran. He ran down the hill like his life depended on it, because it did, and he didn't stop until he was pounding on the Welshman's door.
"Who's there?"
"Huckleberry Finn! Let me in—quick!"
"Huckleberry Finn? That's not a name that opens many doors. But let him in, boys."
Huck tumbled inside, gasping. "Please don't ever tell I was the one who told. He'd kill me. But the Widow Douglas—Injun Joe—he's up there right now—he's going to—"
The old Welshman didn't need to hear more. He grabbed his gun, woke his two grown sons, and within three minutes they were charging up the hill.
Huck hid behind a boulder and listened. For a terrible stretch of silence, nothing happened. Then—BANG! BANG! Gunshots split the night, followed by shouts and the sound of men crashing through the bushes.
Huck didn't wait to find out what happened. He ran back down the hill and didn't stop until he was three miles away.

21.title: LOST IN THE CAVE
21.text:
Deep under the earth, Tom and Becky had lost all sense of time.
They had tried every passage they could find. They had shouted until their voices were raw. They had listened for sounds of rescue and heard nothing but the echo of their own desperate calls, bouncing off the stone walls like mocking laughter.
Their candles were almost gone.
Tom had started with one whole candle and three or four pieces. He blew out Becky's candle to save it. She understood what that meant, and the last bit of hope drained from her face.
They found a small spring and sat down beside it. Both were exhausted—legs shaking, stomachs gnawing with hunger.
"Tom, they'll come looking for us, won't they?"
"Of course they will."
"When will they notice we're gone?"
"When everybody gets back to the boat, I expect."
Becky's face changed. "Tom—it might be dark by then. Would they even notice?"
Tom couldn't answer that. And then a worse thought struck both of them at the same time: Becky was supposed to be staying at Susy Harper's house. Her mother wouldn't miss her until morning. Maybe not even until Sunday afternoon.
They stared at their dying candle. The flame sputtered, rose one last time, and went out.
Total darkness. The blackest dark either of them had ever known.
Becky cried. Tom held her. Time stopped having any meaning. They slept and woke and slept again. They had no idea if it was day or night, Sunday or Monday. Tom had one piece of cake in his pocket—their "wedding cake" from the day they got engaged. They split it. It only made them hungrier.
Tom said they had to keep moving. He had a kite string in his pocket—he tied one end to a rock and played it out as they crept forward, so they could find their way back to the spring if they needed to.
He explored one passage, then another. At the end of the third passage, just as the string was about to run out, he reached around a corner—
And saw a hand holding a candle.
Tom shouted with joy—and then the person stepped into view.
It was Injun Joe.
Tom's heart stopped. For one frozen moment, the two stared at each other. Then Joe turned and ran, disappearing into the darkness.
Tom stood paralyzed. But something was strange—Joe had run away. The echoes must have disguised Tom's voice. Joe didn't know who had shouted. He was hiding in this cave too.
Tom crept back to Becky but didn't tell her who he'd seen. He said he'd shouted "just for luck." They rested again, sleeping fitfully, tortured by hunger and thirst and the absolute, crushing darkness.
When Becky woke, she told Tom to go on without her. She was too weak. She would wait here and die—it wouldn't take long.
Tom kissed her forehead and promised he would come back for her. He took the kite string and crawled into the blackness, feeling his way along the stone walls with his hands and knees.

22.title: ESCAPE
22.text:
Tom followed the passage for what felt like hours. He played out the kite string as far as it would reach, then kept going on his hands and knees. He tried one tunnel, then another, then a third.
At the very end of the third passage, he was about to turn back when he saw something—a tiny speck of light. Faint, far away, but unmistakable.
He dropped the string and crawled toward it. The speck grew larger. Brighter. He pushed his head and shoulders through a narrow hole in the rock—
And there was the Mississippi River, rolling broad and blue in the sunshine.
He could have cried. He nearly did.
He squeezed through the hole, ran back into the cave, and found Becky. She didn't believe him at first—she'd given up. But he pulled her to her feet, half-carried her through the passages, and brought her to the opening. When she saw daylight, she nearly collapsed.
They crawled out onto a bluff overlooking the river. It was late afternoon. They were filthy, starving, and five miles downstream from the cave entrance. Some men in a rowboat came along, and Tom hailed them. The men didn't believe their story at first—"You're five miles below the cave," they said—but they took the children aboard, fed them, and brought them home.
The town went wild. Bells rang. People poured into the streets. The news spread like fire. A procession of villagers filed through Judge Thatcher's house, weeping and laughing and hugging the children. Aunt Polly alternated between squeezing Tom and thanking God.
Tom and Becky spent the rest of the week in bed, recovering. Three days and nights in the cave had nearly killed them.
About two weeks later, Tom went to visit Huck, who had been sick with fever ever since the night he'd saved the Widow Douglas. Tom had plenty to tell him, but first he stopped at Judge Thatcher's house to see Becky.
The Judge mentioned casually that no one would ever get lost in McDougal's Cave again.
"Why not?" Tom asked.
"Because I had the big door sealed with iron plating two weeks ago, and triple-locked. I've got the keys right here."
Tom went white as paper.
"What's the matter, boy? Someone get him some water!"
"Judge—Injun Joe is in the cave!"

23.title: INJUN JOE'S FATE
23.text:
