Within minutes, the news was everywhere. A dozen boats launched from the riverbank. The ferryboat followed, packed with people. Tom Sawyer rode in the lead boat with Judge Thatcher.
When they unlocked the heavy iron door of the cave, they found Injun Joe lying on the ground just inside. He was dead.
His face was pressed against the crack at the bottom of the door, as if his eyes had been fixed on the light of the outside world until the very last moment. His bowie knife lay beside him, its blade broken in half. He'd spent his final days hacking at the great oak beam of the door—useless work, since solid rock formed the threshold beneath it. Even if he'd cut through the wood, he could never have squeezed under.
He had eaten every candle stub left by tourists. He had caught and eaten bats. Near the door, a stalagmite rose from the floor, and on top of it he had placed a hollow stone to catch water that dripped from above—one drop every three minutes. A single spoonful every twenty-four hours.
Tom stared at the cup and the slow-falling drop, and he understood exactly how the man had suffered—because he had nearly suffered the same way.
Injun Joe was buried near the mouth of the cave. People came from miles around for the funeral. Some said they felt sorry for him. Tom did not feel sorry for him. He felt an overwhelming, bone-deep sense of relief.
The weight he'd been carrying since that night in the graveyard was finally gone.
The morning after the funeral, Tom pulled Huck to a private spot.
"Huck, that money was never in Room Number Two at the tavern."
"What?"
"The money is in the cave."
Huck's eyes went wide. "Say that again."
"The treasure—the box of gold—it's in the cave. I know exactly where."
"Tom, are you serious?"
"Dead serious. Will you come with me to get it?"
"I will if we can find it without getting lost."
"I can take us right to it."
They borrowed a small rowboat and headed downriver. Tom found the opening he'd escaped through—hidden behind a thick clump of bushes on the bluff. They crawled in with candles, kite string, and a couple of cloth bags.
Tom led the way through the dark passages until they reached the spot where he'd seen Injun Joe's candle. On a big rock nearby, someone had traced a cross in candle smoke.
"There it is, Huck. 'Number Two—under the cross.' This is where Joe kept his camp."
They searched around the base of the rock and found a blanket, some gnawed bones, and a narrow opening hidden behind a stone. Tom squeezed through and held up his candle.
The treasure box sat in a small natural vault, along with an empty powder keg, a couple of old guns, and some moccasins. Huck plunged his hands into the tarnished gold coins.
"We're rich, Tom! We're actually rich!"
The box weighed about fifty pounds. They stuffed the coins into their bags, dragged everything back through the cave, loaded it into the boat, and rowed home.
They hid the bags in the Widow Douglas's woodshed and set off toward town—but on the way, they ran into the old Welshman, who grabbed them and marched them straight to the Widow Douglas's front door.
"The widow's throwing a party," he said. "You two are coming."
"But we're filthy—"
"There are clean clothes inside. Get dressed."

24.title: TOM SAWYER'S GANG
24.text:
The Widow Douglas's parlor was full of people—the Thatchers, the Harpers, Aunt Polly, Sid, Mary, the minister, and half the town. The boys were scrubbed and stuffed into new suits. Huck kept eyeing the window, calculating the drop.
"Tom, we can escape if we—"
"Relax. It's just a party."
The Welshman stood up and made a speech, thanking the widow for her hospitality and revealing that it was Huckleberry Finn who had saved her from Injun Joe that night on Cardiff Hill. Huck turned crimson. The widow said she meant to give Huck a permanent home, educate him, and eventually set him up in business.
Tom couldn't wait another second. He stood up and said: "Huck doesn't need charity. Huck's rich."
Nobody laughed—they assumed it was a joke. Tom ran outside, came back dragging a small wagon, and dumped two bags of gold coins onto the table.
"Half is Huck's and half is mine!"
The room went dead silent. Then everyone started talking at once. Tom told the whole story—the haunted house, Injun Joe, Number Two under the cross, the treasure box in the cave. When he was finished, the money was counted.
Twelve thousand dollars. More than anyone in the room had ever seen.
The Widow Douglas invested Huck's share. Judge Thatcher did the same with Tom's, at Aunt Polly's request. Each boy had an income of about a dollar a day—a fortune for the time.
Judge Thatcher said Tom was the bravest boy he'd ever met. When Becky told her father how Tom had taken her whipping at school, the Judge said it was "a noble and generous act" and that Tom would make a great man someday.
Huck's new life, however, was not going smoothly. The Widow Douglas was kind, but her world had rules. He had to eat with a fork. He had to wear shoes. He had to sleep in sheets. He had to go to church. He couldn't smoke, couldn't swear, couldn't spit. Everything was scheduled—up by a bell, meals by a bell, bed by a bell.
After three miserable weeks, Huck disappeared.
The whole town searched for him. Tom Sawyer found him on the second day—hiding in an empty barrel behind the old slaughter-house, happily smoking a pipe in his old rags.
"Don't talk about it, Tom. I've tried it and it doesn't work. The widow means well, but I can't stand the rules. I've got to get up at the same time every morning. I've got to wash and comb my hair. I can't fish unless I ask, can't swim unless I ask. I've got to wear shoes every Sunday. Being rich isn't what it's cracked up to be—it's just worry and work and wishing you were dead. Take my share of the money and leave me alone."
"I can't do that, Huck."
"Why not?"
"Because if you're not respectable, you can't be in the gang."
"What gang?"
"Tom Sawyer's Gang. I'm starting a gang of robbers, and we need a proper hideout, and rules, and an oath signed in blood at midnight. But we can't let you in if you're not respectable."
Huck thought about this for a long time.
"All right," he finally said. "I'll go back to the widow for a month and try to stand it—if you let me in the gang."
"Done!"
"When do we start?"
"Right away. We'll get the boys together tonight for the initiation. We'll swear the oath in the cave at midnight."
"That sounds perfect, Tom."
"It will be. And Huck—I'll talk to the widow. I'll get her to ease up a little."
"Would you? That'd help. If she'd just let me smoke and swear in private, I think I could make it."
Tom grinned. "Come on, old friend. Let's go home."
And so they did.
THE END
